title: Fitting wall tiles in a running bond pattern
subtitle: Lay ceramic or porcelain tiles in a staggered brick-style layout on a wall
excerpt: How to lay ceramic or porcelain wall tiles in a running bond (brick bond) pattern, where each tile is offset by half its length from the row above and below. Covers setting out the starting point to avoid thin cut tiles at the edges, cutting tiles to maintain the offset, and grouting the staggered joints.
sourceNotes: BS 5385-1: Wall and floor tiling. Tile Association installation guidance (open-access). City and Guilds Level 2 Tiling. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A tile layout pattern in which each row is offset by half a tile length from the rows above and below it, mimicking the appearance of traditional brickwork. Also called a brick bond or a half-offset pattern. The staggered joint lines distribute load in masonry and create a visually dynamic pattern in tiling. Setting out is more critical than for a grid pattern because the offset must be maintained consistently and cut tiles at borders must align symmetrically.

content.text: A running bond looks clean when set out right and untidy when it is not. Unlike a grid, the set-out must work in two directions at once. The first tile in the bottom row fixes the offset for every row above it. Any drift from a true half-offset will show the full height of the wall. The setting-out step takes longer than the tiling itself; this is normal.

layout looks clean and deliberate when set out well and untidy when it is not. The key difference from a grid layout is that the set-out must be done in two dimensions simultaneously: the first tile in the bottom row determines the offset of every subsequent row, and any variation from a true half-offset is visible along the whole height of the wall. The setting-out step takes more time than the tiling itself; this is normal.
Step 1, set out the horizontal starting point

Find the centre of the wall and mark a vertical centre line with the spirit level. Count how many tiles fit across from the centre line to each side. If the last tile on each side is less than half a tile wide, shift the starting position by half a tile so the cut tiles at both borders are larger than half. This is the same set-out calculation as for a grid pattern, but the running bond introduces a second consideration: in every other row, the cut tile at the border will be the half-offset of the tile above or below. Calculate both rows before fixing the starting point.
Step 2, establish a level datum and start tiling

Fix a temporary timber batten to the wall at the height of the first full row above any cut row at the base. Check it is level with the spirit level along its full length. Start the first row against this batten at the horizontal centre position, laying whole tiles outward to each side. The first row is the grid row; alternate rows will be the half-offset row, starting half a tile length from the centre.
Step 3, lay alternate rows with the half offset

Apply adhesive with the notched edge on sections of approximately 1 m². Place the second row tiles starting at the centre of the first-row tile below, maintaining the half-offset consistently. Use tile spacers at every joint, including the vertical joints; the spacer size determines the joint width. Grout joint width between 2 mm and 3 mm works well for standard ceramic; smaller joints emphasise the brick pattern.

Measure the required cut size for each border tile individually; the offset means alternate rows have different cut widths. Cut tiles with the angle grinder fitted with a diamond blade using a straightedge to guide the cut. Mark the cut line on the face of the tile with a pencil, score along the line with the utility knife to prevent the glaze chipping at the cut line, then make the cut. Smooth the cut edge on the sanding block before fitting.

Allow the adhesive to cure for 24 hours before grouting. Remove tile spacers. Apply grout with a rubber grout float, pressing it into the joints at a 45° angle to the joint direction. Wipe the tile face clean with a damp sponge before the grout sets on the surface. Allow the grout to haze, then polish with a dry cloth. The running bond pattern emphasises long continuous lines; any variation in grout joint width is visible, so maintain consistent pressure with the float across every joint.

attrs.symptom: The offset drifts over the height of the wall and rows are no longer truly half-offset
attrs.cause: Tiles are not perfectly square, or the spacers were not used consistently in the vertical joints.
attrs.fix: Check each row against the starting datum with the tape measure before fixing it. If tiles are slightly non-square, adjust the spacer sizes in the vertical joints to compensate and re-establish the half-offset. Once adhesive has set, drift is visible and requires re-tiling the affected rows.

attrs.symptom: Very thin slivers of tile appear at some border positions
attrs.cause: The set-out calculation was not done for both row offsets before starting.
attrs.fix: This is usually a set-out error. If tiling has started and a thin sliver is unavoidable, the cleanest fix is to use the same small tile on both sides of the wall (accepting a deliberate design decision) rather than have a large tile on one side and a sliver on the other.
